claims.claim: For the KULR 21700-K500, nail penetration trigger resulted in an average of 12.3% higher heat output compared to Heater (ISC) and 16.8% higher compared to Heater (Non-ISC).
claims.quote: The KULR 21700-K500 appeared to have an average of 12.3 % higher heat output for Nail Penetration vs. Heater (ISC) and a 16.8 % higher for Nail Penetration vs. heater (Non-ISC).

claims.claim: For the Saft 33600-VES16, nail penetration trigger resulted in an average of 18.1% higher heat output compared to Heater (ISC) and 28.4% higher compared to Heater (Non-ISC).
claims.quote: The Saft 33600-VES16 had an average of 18.1 % higher heat output for Nail Penetration trigger vs. Heater (ISC) trigger and 28.4 % higher for Nail Penetration trigger vs. Heater (Non-ISC) trigger.

claims.claim: The difference in heat output between nail penetration and thermal abuse increases with increasing cell diameter, hypothesized to be caused by nail penetration immediately initiating thermal runaway across every punctured layer while thermal abuse causes slower radial propagation from layer to layer.
claims.quote: These results quantitatively show that the difference in heat output between nail and thermal abuse increases with increasing diameter of cell. This is hypothesized to be caused by an increase in the rate of thermal runaway propagation for nail penetration tests for larger diameter cells. This is due to the nail immediately initiating thermal runaway across every layer that it punctures, from the outer layer near the casing, to the inner layer at the center of the cell. Whereas during thermal abuse the radial propagation occurs in slower steps from layer to layer.

claims.claim: The KULR 18650-K330 did not show significant variation in total heat output as a function of trigger method, suggesting worst-case scenario heat output is achievable in assembly level testing regardless of trigger method for this cell format.
claims.quote: There was not a significant difference in total heat output as a function of trigger method for the KULR 18650-K330... Additionally, the KULR 18650-K330 results suggest that worst-case scenario heat output is achievable in assembly level testing regardless of the utilized trigger method.

claims.claim: Nail penetration tended to result in the highest fraction of energy released through the cell body compared to other trigger methods, possibly because the nail traps electrode winding material inside the cell casing forcing decomposition reactions to occur within the casing.
claims.quote: Other than for the KULR 21700-K500, nail penetration tended to result in the highest fraction of energy being released through the cell body. This is possibly because the nail traps a portion of the electrode winding assembly inside of the cell casing, which forces the decomposition reactions to occur within the casing, which in turn means that a higher fraction of the total energy will be released through the cell body.

claims.claim: The average threshold valve opening pressure for the safety vent in the tested 18650 NCA cells was approximately 1.69 MPa.
claims.quote: The averaged repeated testing results indicated that the threshold valve opening pressure vP was ~1.69 MPa (Table 3).

claims.claim: The average burst pressure of the 18650 NCA cell shell when the safety valve was sealed was approximately 6.78 MPa.
claims.quote: The average burst pressure fP was measured as ~6.78 MPa (Table 3) (define the facture pressure as fP).

claims.claim: Adding a tight-fitting CFRP sleeve around the cell effectively prevented sidewall rupture during thermal runaway, with no sidewall rupture observed in any of the five tested samples with CFRP.
claims.quote: The TR tests of samples with the CFRP layer suggest that the cell sidewall rupture can be effectively limited by the CFRP layer: there was no sidewall rupture observed.

claims.claim: The simulated fracture pressure of the battery shell with a CFRP sleeve (~7.07 MPa) was lower than the simulated fracture pressure of the shell without a CFRP sleeve (~8.79 MPa).
claims.quote: The simulation results indicate that fP of the battery shell with CFRP is ~7.07 MPa, which is smaller than fP of the battery shell without CFRP (8.79 MPa).

claims.claim: When shell thickness decreases below 0.08 mm, the deformation mode changes from an axial dent to a severe 'X' shape, because decreasing thickness reduces the in-plane critical buckling force.
claims.quote: For shells thickness larger than 0.08 mm, the deformation is a kind of axial direction dent. However, when the thickness decreases to 0.04 mm, the deformation changes to an 'X' shape and becomes very severe. This is because when thickness decreases, the in-plane critical force for buckling of the shell decreases.

claims.claim: Under uniform temperature distribution during thermal runaway, shell fracture tends to occur at the edge of the battery end cap due to stress concentration from abrupt geometry change, whereas under laterally concentrated temperature distribution, fracture originates at the point of highest heat concentration and propagates outward.
claims.quote: The strain distribution is uniform due to the uniform temperature distribution, and the rupture occurred near the edge of the cap with the maximum stress concentration due to the abrupt geometry shape change... For the Type II uneven distribution case, the fracture originates at the point of highest heat concentration and then propagates to the surrounding area, representing the side rupture scenario.

claims.claim: The axial dent observed on cells with CFRP sleeves during thermal runaway is caused by uneven temperature distribution: high-temperature areas soften the steel shell, producing unrecoverable plastic strain, while the CFRP layer prevents outward expansion, forcing the shell to bend inward.
claims.quote: When the temperature increased, plastic strain and deformation occurred in the high-temperature area... The plastic strain and deformation are unrecoverable. However, the CFRP layer prevent the outward expansion therefore the steel stretching had to bend inward. The axial dent was finally produced. In this case, the uneven temperature distribution further leads to the occurrence of an axial dent.

claims.claim: Lithium-ion batteries (NMC811/SiC, 21700 and 18650 formats) reached venting gas temperatures exceeding 1370°C during thermal runaway, which is the measurement limit of the thermocouple used.
claims.quote: During testing, the venting gas temperature of LIBs exceeded 1000°C and, in some tests, reached values up to 1370°C, which is the measurement limit of the thermocouple's measurement capability. However, the actual venting gas temperature may have been even higher.

claims.claim: Sodium-ion batteries (NaMn1/3Fe1/3Ni1/3O2 cathode, 18650 format) exhibited peak venting gas temperatures not exceeding 350°C during thermal runaway, with no gas ignition observed.
claims.quote: The measured peak venting gas temperatures of the 18 650 SIBs were significantly lower, with values not exceeding 350°C. The lower temperature measured compared to LIBs may be connected to the lower energy stored in the investigated SIBs... Our measurement results are supported by videos, where it can be observed that there was no ignition or fire in tested SIBs, leading to measuring lower gas temperatures compared to both LIB types.

claims.claim: Peak mass flow rate during thermal runaway reached 2000 g/s for 18650 LIBs and 1500 g/s for 21700 LIBs, but these peak values lasted only several milliseconds.
claims.quote: The peak values of the mass flow rate were even higher - 1500 g s−1 for 21 700 LIBs and 2000 g s−1 for 18 650 LIBs but lasting only several milliseconds.

claims.claim: The average total mass loss during thermal runaway was 77% for 21700 LIBs and 75% for 18650 LIBs, compared to only 55% for 18650 SIBs.
claims.quote: The average total mass loss for 21 700 LIBs is 77 % and for 18 650 LIBs it is 75 %... Compared to both LIBs, the average total mass loss of the investigated SIBs in 18 650 format is only 55 %.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway start temperature of investigated 18650 SIBs was on average 10°C to 15°C higher than that of investigated 18650 LIBs, suggesting SIBs have a larger safety window before TR onset.
claims.quote: Compared to LIBs, the TR start temperature of investigated SIBs is, on average, 10°C to 15°C higher, and, thus, the TR occurred later despite the same heating rate, indicating that investigated SIBs could be more thermally stable compared to investigated LIBs.

claims.claim: The venting gas velocity of LIBs (both 21700 and 18650 formats) exceeded 100 m/s at the beginning of thermal runaway and decreased below 50 m/s after 200 ms, exhibiting an exponential decay over time.
claims.quote: The high velocities exceeding 100 m s−1 at the beginning of TR can be attributed to the gas ignition. The venting gas velocity decreases after 200 ms, falling below 50 m s−1 in both LIB formats and further drops below the 25 m s−1 value after 500 ms since the beginning of the optically visible TR event.

claims.claim: The absence of gas ignition in investigated SIBs precluded optical estimation of venting gas velocity and mass flow rate calculation, representing a limitation of the methodology for SIB characterization.
claims.quote: The mass flow rate for the 18 650 SIBs could not be estimated due to the absence of ignition and the venting gas velocity values... The investigation of SIBs in the 18 650 format using optical velocity estimation was impossible since the vented gas did not ignite in any of the three tested cells.
